title: Access Control Strategies
description: Examine three primary strategies for user access in Windchill, compare access control priorities, and review the Windchill domain architecture.

topics.title: Understanding Access Control in Windchill

content.text: Administrators can control user and group access in Windchill using three strategies that can be combined to define proper object access control. Understanding how these strategies interact is essential for building a secure and manageable access control schema.

rows: All objects of a class within a context
Access, notification, and indexing rules applied to a storage context. Rules apply broadly to all objects of the same type.
Life cycle and object-level permissions. Can only grant access, never deny. Supersedes domain policy access control rules.
Classify objects with labels (e.g., Top Secret, Restricted). Denies all access to unauthorized users, regardless of any other rules.

content.text: Domain policies establish the default behavior for all objects of a type. Ad hoc permissions provide exceptions for specific objects. Security labels provide the strongest possible restriction, overriding everything.

When planning your access control strategy, use policy rules to establish default behavior for specific object types. Ad hoc and life cycle access control rules should be used sparingly to limit complexity and administration effort.

Access control directly related to a business process should use life cycle ad hoc rules. Access control at the discretion of administrators or object creators should use the Edit Access Control interface.

content.prompt: Match each access control scenario to the appropriate strategy:

pairs.left: All engineers can read CAD documents in a product
Grant a specific user edit access to one document
Restrict a classified document to authorized personnel only
Allow reviewers to read documents during the review process

keyTakeaways: Three strategies combine to define access control: domain policies, ad hoc permissions, and security labels.
Domain policies apply broadly to all objects of a class; ad hoc permissions target individual objects.
Security labels are the strongest control, denying all access to unauthorized users regardless of other rules.
Use domain policies for defaults, ad hoc for exceptions, and security labels for classified content.

topics.title: Access Control Priorities

content.text: Windchill resolves access by applying the strongest rule that applies to the object. Understanding the priority order is critical for predicting how rules interact and ensuring users get the correct access.

rows: Denies access to any unauthorized user - no other rule can override
Cannot be overridden by any grant rule, ad hoc, or user permission
Object-level grant that overrides domain-level deny (but not security labels or absolute deny)
Grant to the Owner pseudo-role - supersedes group-level deny
Deny to a named user takes precedence over grant to the same user
Rules for groups, context roles, organization roles, and the All pseudo-role
If no rule is set, access is denied by default

content.text: The main point to remember is that the strongest rule that applies takes precedence, regardless of where in the domain, context, or object hierarchy it originates.
If the security label denies the user and there is no security agreement, there is no reason to review other rules. Absolute deny permissions are similarly absolute - no grant rule is stronger.

content.prompt: Arrange these access control rules from strongest to weakest:
items: Domain grant to a group of users

keyTakeaways: The strongest rule that applies to an object takes precedence, regardless of its origin.
Security labels are the strongest rule - they cannot be overridden by any other access control mechanism.
Absolute deny domain rules cannot be overridden by ad hoc rules or user-specific grants.
If no rule is set for a participant, access is denied by default.

topics.title: Windchill Context and Domain Architecture

content.text: Windchill system behavior is based on a hierarchical context model. The Site context represents the system, organizations are components of the Site, and products, libraries, projects, and programs are components within an organization. Domains and cabinets are connected to this hierarchy.

rows: Stores user's notebook, checked-out copies, workspaces
Controls access, indexing, and notification rules for objects

content.text: All domains are children of the /(Root) domain, which establishes rules governing the entire Windchill installation. The domain hierarchy includes specialized domains for different purposes.

cards.back: Control participants (users, groups, organizations). It is recommended not to alter these domains as the defaults are needed for participants to operate correctly.
Associated with System cabinets, controlling administrative templates. Every context has a system domain. Do not alter or add rules to these domains.
Govern access, notification, and indexing for data objects. This is where business administrators concentrate most domain administration efforts.
Bypasses global rules from Default and PDM domains. Used when a product or library is set to private, enabling restrictive access rules.

content.text: As a business administrator, concentrate most of your domain administration efforts on the data domains (Default, PDM, Project). Avoid modifying User, System, and special Site domains.

content.prompt: Match each domain type to its primary purpose:

pairs.left: Stores rules for parts, documents, and CAD files
Controls life cycle and workflow templates
Bypasses global PDM rules for restricted contexts
Parent to all public Product and Library domains

keyTakeaways: Windchill uses a hierarchical context model with the /(Root) domain at the top governing all domains.
Every access-controlled object is associated with exactly one administrative domain.
Business administrators should focus on data domains (Default, PDM, Project) and avoid modifying User and System domains.
The Private domain bypasses global rules, enabling restrictive access for sensitive contexts.

topics.title: Review Domain Architecture

content.text: In this exercise, you review the domains available in the Windchill installation using the Policy Administrator utility. You will explore the domain hierarchy and understand how different domains are structured.
You will sign in as business administrator bpatterson/ptc to access the Policy Administrator.

content.title: Review Domain Architecture
content.objective: Access the Policy Administrator, explore the domain hierarchy, and identify the purpose of each domain level.

steps.action: Launch a browser and click the Windchill bookmark.
steps.detail: This opens the Windchill home page.
The sign-in page allows you to authenticate with your Windchill credentials.

steps.action: Sign in to Windchill with username bpatterson and password ptc.
steps.detail: bpatterson is a business administrator with access to the Policy Administrator utility.
The Navigator panel provides access to organizational contexts and utilities.
The Utilities section contains administration tools including Policy Administration.
The Policy Administrator opens in a new tab, displaying the domain hierarchy and rule tables.

steps.action: In the Policy Administration tab, select the / (Root) domain name.
steps.detail: The Root domain contains rules that govern the entire Windchill installation.

steps.action: Below the Root, expand the Default node.
steps.detail: This domain governs policies for PTC Publications data.
This is the parent of all Product and Library public default domains. It is different from the Organization domain.

This is the parent of Product and Project domains wherein you can create rules that affect all contexts in that Organization. Note that one rule exists here to permit all PTC Publications members to create projects.
This domain can bypass any global rules created at the Organization level in favor of very restrictive access rules.
This domain controls administrative templates based on the hierarchy of domains.
steps.hint: Hover over the rows in the Type column in the Search Results table to see the object types.

steps.detail: The User domain is the parent for organization and unaffiliated participant domains.
This is the parent organization for all participants in PTC Publications.
Note that user bpatterson cannot view the domains in other organization contexts.
You have completed the review of the domain architecture.

keyTakeaways: The Policy Administrator is accessed from the Utilities section of an organization context.
The Root domain contains rules governing the entire Windchill installation.
The PDM domain is the parent of all public Product and Library default domains.
User and System domains should not be modified - they contain essential default rules.
